stopConditions: Only stage short evidence snippets; do not publish raw HTML, PDF text, screenshots, or full source text.
Treat source-health timestamps as metadata only; do not upgrade claim review state from source metadata.

methodsAttempted.notes: HTTP-first maintenance scan identified high-confidence content_policy_delta candidates from promoted official source URLs.
Codex local reviewer compared candidate snippets against current public claims and promoted only non-duplicate claim expansions.

artifacts.summary: Local maintenance review converted six non-duplicate candidate policy deltas into claim/evidence release artifacts. UWA candidate was not promoted because current public claims already cover the tier, permission, and acknowledgement facts.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Generative AI | HMS IT

artifacts.claimText: Harvard Medical School responsible AI guidance says users should verify outputs, uphold academic integrity, be transparent about acceptable use, and cite AI contributions appropriately in research and academic work.
artifacts.normalizedValue: HMS responsible AI guidance requires verification, academic-integrity alignment, transparency, and appropriate citation of AI contributions.

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Verify outputs. Uphold academic integrity. Be transparent with students and colleagues about acceptable use. Cite AI contributions appropriately in research and academic work.

artifacts.sourceTitle: GenAI and Assessment

artifacts.claimText: Trinity College Dublin says specific uses of GenAI in an assessment must be disclosed, properly acknowledged, and referenced; students should explain what, when, and how they used GenAI.
artifacts.normalizedValue: GenAI assessment use must be disclosed, acknowledged, referenced, and explained.

citation.publisher: Trinity College Dublin, The University of Dublin
citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.sourceTitle: GenAI and Assessment
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: To maintain academic integrity when using GenAI in an assessment, the specific uses of GenAI must be disclosed, properly acknowledged and referenced. Students should explain in detail what, when, and how they used GenAI in their assignment.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Assessment and Academic Integrity / disclosure guidance

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidance for Faculty Use of AI in Grading and Assessment

artifacts.claimText: UNC-Chapel Hill says faculty should clearly explain when and how AI tools are used in assessment evaluation, including what comes from AI and what comes from instructor judgment.
artifacts.normalizedValue: Faculty should disclose AI use in assessment evaluation and distinguish AI-supported evaluation from instructor judgment.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidance for Faculty Use of AI in Grading and Assessment
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Faculty should clearly explain to students when and how AI tools are used in support of assessment evaluation. Students should understand which parts of their evaluation come from AI and which come from the instructor's own judgment.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Student Learning Assessment Policy at King Saud University 2025/1447AH

artifacts.claimText: King Saud University's assessment policy says the university supports using generative AI tools in education while emphasizing knowledge, skills, and awareness needed to regulate use and understand AI-generated information or data.
artifacts.normalizedValue: KSU assessment policy supports educational GenAI use while emphasizing knowledge, skills, and awareness for regulated use.

citation.citationTitle: Student Learning Assessment Policy at King Saud University 2025/1447AH
citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Student Learning Assessment Policy at King Saud University 2025/1447AH
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: The university supports the use of these tools, emphasizing: The importance of learning certain knowledge, skills, and understanding that help regulate the use of generative AI in certain situations, and understanding the information or data generated by generative AI.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Using digital technology in assessment / Generative AI

artifacts.sourceTitle: Artificial Intelligence in Classes

artifacts.claimText: UCSB guidance says instructors using AI to identify patterns in student work by uploading that work should obtain student permission and use only campus-approved AI platforms.
artifacts.normalizedValue: Uploading student work to AI for pattern identification requires student permission and campus-approved AI platforms.

artifacts.claimText: Ohio State Arts and Sciences guidance provides sample course-level prohibition language stating students are not permitted to use AI tools in a course when that is the instructor's policy.
artifacts.normalizedValue: OSU ASC guidance offers course-level no-AI sample policy language.

citation.citationTitle: Crafting Policy for Student Use of Artificial Intelligence | ASC Office of Distance Education
citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Crafting Policy for Student Use of Artificial Intelligence | ASC Office of Distance Education
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Course-level Prohibition of Student Use of AI If students may not make any use of AI in your course, we recommend a statement along the following lines: Because you will best accomplish the goals of this course by undertaking all activities and assessments without AI assistance, you are not permitted to use any AI tools in this course.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Course-level Prohibition of Student Use of AI
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.claimText: Ohio State guidance says instructors who permit AI should provide clear and precise parameters about tools, features, assignments, activities, and allowable use.
artifacts.normalizedValue: OSU ASC guidance recommends precise course parameters when AI use is permitted.

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Crafting Policy for Student Use of Artificial Intelligence | ASC Office of Distance Education
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: If you want to permit students to use AI, it is important to provide clear and precise parameters about the kind of use you will permit, in order to avoid misunderstandings. This may vary by assignment and activity, and type of AI tool.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Course-level Permission for Student Use of AI
